bugs.description: The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b‑sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct powers of two) the algorithm does not finish within the time limit, causing a TLE. The solution is therefore not guaranteed to run within the required limits.
bugs.actual_behavior: Program exceeds the time limit (does not terminate in reasonable time).

bugs.description: The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b‑sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct powers of two) the algorithm does not finish within the time limit, causing a TLE. The solution is therefore not guaranteed to run within the required limits.
bugs.actual_behavior: Program exceeds the time limit (does not terminate in reasonable time).

bugs.bug_description: The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b-sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct powers of two) the algorithm does not finish within the time limit, causing a TLE. The solution is therefore not guaranteed to run within the required limits.
bugs.reasoning: The algorithm performs a depth‑first search with branching factor roughly O(|b|·n) at each level and depth up to n‑1 (max 9). In the worst case where no solution exists, it may explore an exponential number of states (approximately up to 200^9 in the worst‑case scenario). This far exceeds the allowed time for the given constraints (t ≤ 20, n ≤ 10). Therefore the code can TLE on inputs such as the provided powers‑of‑two test, confirming the bug.

evaluations.bug_description: The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b-sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct powers of two) the algorithm does not finish within the time limit, causing a TLE. The solution is therefore not guaranteed to run within the required limits.
evaluations.reasoning: The algorithm performs a depth‑first search with branching factor roughly O(|b|·n) at each level and depth up to n‑1 (max 9). In the worst case where no solution exists, it may explore an exponential number of states (approximately up to 200^9 in the worst‑case scenario). This far exceeds the allowed time for the given constraints (t ≤ 20, n ≤ 10). Therefore the code can TLE on inputs such as the provided powers‑of‑two test, confirming the bug.

bugs.description: The recursive DFS explores an exponential number of candidate b‑sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct values) the search tree grows roughly O((|b|·n)^{n}) and the algorithm does not finish within the time limit, causing a TLE. No memoization or pruning is used, so the solution is not guaranteed to run within the required limits.
bugs.actual_behavior: Program exceeds the time limit (does not terminate in reasonable time).

bugs.description: The recursive DFS explores an exponential number of candidate b‑sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct values) the search tree grows roughly O((|b|·n)^{n}) and the algorithm does not finish within the time limit, causing a TLE. No memoization or pruning is used, so the solution is not guaranteed to run within the required limits.
bugs.actual_behavior: Program exceeds the time limit (does not terminate in reasonable time).

bugs.bug_description: The recursive DFS explores an exponential number of candidate b‑sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct values) the search tree grows roughly O((|b|·n)^{n}) and the algorithm does not finish within the time limit, causing a TLE. No memoization or pruning is used, so the solution is not guaranteed to run within the required limits.
bugs.reasoning: The algorithm starts with a single element {0} and at each recursion step generates up to 2·|b|·n new candidates (x±a_i for each x∈b and each a_i). With n≤10, the depth of recursion is n‑1≤9, so the branching factor can be as high as ~200. In the worst case the search explores a tree of size roughly 200^9, which is astronomically large. The code has no memoization or pruning to avoid revisiting the same b‑sets, so for inputs where no solution exists (e.g., the provided test case of powers of two) it will exhaust the search space and never terminate within typical time limits. Hence the claim of a time‑limit‑exceeded bug is valid.

evaluations.bug_description: The recursive DFS explores an exponential number of candidate b‑sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct values) the search tree grows roughly O((|b|·n)^{n}) and the algorithm does not finish within the time limit, causing a TLE. No memoization or pruning is used, so the solution is not guaranteed to run within the required limits.
evaluations.reasoning: The algorithm starts with a single element {0} and at each recursion step generates up to 2·|b|·n new candidates (x±a_i for each x∈b and each a_i). With n≤10, the depth of recursion is n‑1≤9, so the branching factor can be as high as ~200. In the worst case the search explores a tree of size roughly 200^9, which is astronomically large. The code has no memoization or pruning to avoid revisiting the same b‑sets, so for inputs where no solution exists (e.g., the provided test case of powers of two) it will exhaust the search space and never terminate within typical time limits. Hence the claim of a time‑limit‑exceeded bug is valid.

bugs.description: The recursive DFS explores an exponential number of candidate b‑sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct values) the search tree grows astronomically large, causing the program to exceed the time limit (TLE). No memoization or pruning is used, so the algorithm is not guaranteed to finish within the required limits.
bugs.fix: Replace the exponential DFS with a polynomial‑time DP. The problem can be reduced to checking whether a non‑empty signed subset of the a_i sums to zero (allowing each a_i to be used with coefficient -1, 0, or +1). Use a DP set of reachable sums, iterating over each a_i and updating the set with sums +/- a_i. If zero becomes reachable (other than the trivial all‑zero choice) or any a_i is zero, answer YES; otherwise NO. This runs in O(n * S) where S is the total absolute sum (≤ 10^6), easily fast for n ≤ 10.
bugs.reasoning: Both attacker and defender identified the same issue and agreed it is a confirmed bug. The exponential search without pruning leads to TLE on inputs such as the powers‑of‑two test case, confirming the bug's validity.

final_bugs.description: The recursive DFS explores an exponential number of candidate b‑sets. For worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct values) the search tree grows astronomically large, causing the program to exceed the time limit (TLE). No memoization or pruning is used, so the algorithm is not guaranteed to finish within the required limits.
final_bugs.fix: Replace the exponential DFS with a polynomial‑time DP. The problem can be reduced to checking whether a non‑empty signed subset of the a_i sums to zero (allowing each a_i to be used with coefficient -1, 0, or +1). Use a DP set of reachable sums, iterating over each a_i and updating the set with sums +/- a_i. If zero becomes reachable (other than the trivial all‑zero choice) or any a_i is zero, answer YES; otherwise NO. This runs in O(n * S) where S is the total absolute sum (≤ 10^6), easily fast for n ≤ 10.
final_bugs.reasoning: Both attacker and defender identified the same issue and agreed it is a confirmed bug. The exponential search without pruning leads to TLE on inputs such as the powers‑of‑two test case, confirming the bug's validity.

fixes.code:
def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    if all(x == 0 for x in a):
        print("YES")
        return
    
    # Check if any non-empty signed subset sums to 0
    # DP over possible sums
    reachable = {0}
    for ai in a:
        new_reachable = set()
        for s in reachable:
            new_reachable.add(s + ai)
            new_reachable.add(s - ai)
        reachable = reachable | new_reachable
    
    # Remove 0 from initial - we need non-empty subset
    # Actually reachable contains sums of signed subsets including empty (=0)
    # We need to track non-empty separately
    non_empty = set()
    for ai in a:
        new_non_empty = set()
        for s in non_empty:
            new_non_empty.add(s + ai)
            new_non_empty.add(s - ai)
        new_non_empty.add(ai)
        new_non_empty.add(-ai)
        non_empty = non_empty | new_non_empty
    
    print("YES" if 0 in non_empty else "NO")

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

pipelines.final_code:
def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    if all(x == 0 for x in a):
        print("YES")
        return
    
    # Check if any non-empty signed subset sums to 0
    # DP over possible sums
    reachable = {0}
    for ai in a:
        new_reachable = set()
        for s in reachable:
            new_reachable.add(s + ai)
            new_reachable.add(s - ai)
        reachable = reachable | new_reachable
    
    # Remove 0 from initial - we need non-empty subset
    # Actually reachable contains sums of signed subsets including empty (=0)
    # We need to track non-empty separately
    non_empty = set()
    for ai in a:
        new_non_empty = set()
        for s in non_empty:
            new_non_empty.add(s + ai)
            new_non_empty.add(s - ai)
        new_non_empty.add(ai)
        new_non_empty.add(-ai)
        non_empty = non_empty | new_non_empty
    
    print("YES" if 0 in non_empty else "NO")

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()
